entries.intro: It rained salt near {subject_focus_the} yesterday. Not a figure of speech: actual salt crystals fell from the sky for about twenty minutes.
details: The ground was white after. Tasted it myself: bitter, gritty, unmistakably salt.
The garden plots are damaged. Anything with shallow roots is wilting already.
The rain came from one cloud. Just one, sitting low and dark while the rest of the sky was clear.
reactions: We're the closest settlement to the coast, but it's still forty miles. Salt doesn't just ride the wind that far in crystal form.
The farmers are trying to flush their soil with fresh water. It's going to be a lean season if they can't.

statCheck.pass: I collected some of the salt and compared it to sea salt and mine salt. It matches neither. The crystal structure is different: more geometric, like it was grown, not evaporated.
statCheck.fail: You taste the residue on a leaf and confirm it's salty, but beyond that it just seems like salt. Nothing you can learn without proper tools.

entries.intro: There's been fog near {subject_focus_the} every morning for three weeks straight. Not unusual on its own, except it doesn't behave like fog.
details: It rolls in from the east at exactly the same time each morning and burns off at exactly the same time. Down to the minute.
Sound doesn't carry through it. Stand ten paces from someone and you can't hear them shout.
reactions: The children play in it, which terrifies their parents. Hard to keep track of someone you can't hear.
I've walked through morning fog my whole life. This fog feels different. Heavier. Like walking through water that isn't wet.

statCheck.pass: I stood at the edge and watched it roll in. It doesn't spread evenly: it follows the road, fills the low ground, and stops at a clean line along the eastern hill. Like it knows where the boundaries are.
statCheck.fail: You stand in the fog and try to observe its behavior, but once you're inside it, visibility drops to nothing. You can't see the edges from within.

entries.intro: Lightning struck the same tree near {subject_focus_the} four nights in a row. The same tree, during four separate storms.
details: The tree is a massive old oak at the top of the south ridge. After four strikes it's still standing, which shouldn't be possible.
Each strike left a different pattern in the bark. The fourth one looks almost like writing, if you squint.
The ground around the base is fused: dirt and stone melted together into a glassy ring.
reactions: The woodcutter says to leave it alone. You don't cut down a tree that lightning keeps choosing.
Some of the children have started calling it the thunder tree. They dare each other to touch it.

entries.intro: The wind near {subject_focus_the} has been blowing in a circle for the past week. Not a storm, not a tornado: just a steady breeze that goes in a loop around the village.
details: If you hang a ribbon anywhere in the village, it points clockwise. Step outside the village boundary and the air is perfectly still.
Smoke from the chimneys spirals instead of rising straight. It makes the whole village look like it's breathing.
reactions: People are used to it now, which worries me more than the wind itself. We shouldn't get comfortable with things that don't make sense.
The miller loves it. His wheel hasn't stopped turning in a week. Best production he's ever had.

statCheck.pass: I walked the boundary where the wind starts. It's a perfect circle, centered on the old well in the village square. Something about that well is anchoring this.
statCheck.fail: You try to map where the wind starts and stops, but it shifts subtly and you lose the pattern. Close, but not precise enough to pin down.

entries.intro: It snowed near {subject_focus_the} for an hour last week. In the middle of summer. Then it stopped and the temperature went right back to normal.
details: Big, heavy flakes. Accumulated about an inch before it quit. Then it melted within the hour.
The snow only fell on the village. The fields on either side stayed dry. Clear boundary, like a wall.
It wasn't cold enough to snow. The air was warm and the snow was warm. It should have been rain, but it wasn't.
reactions: The children loved it. The adults did not.
I caught some on my tongue. It tasted like spring water: clean, sweet, not like normal snow at all. That bothered me more than the snow itself.
